Let $S$ be a set of $2 \times 2$ matrices with complex numbers as entries, and
let $T$ be the subset of $S$ consisting of matrices whose eigenvalues are $\pm
1$ (so the eigenvalues for each matrix in $T$ are $\lbrace 1, 1\rbrace$ or
$\lbrace 1, -1\rbrace$ or $\lbrace -1, -1\rbrace$). Suppose there are exactly
three matrices in $T$. Prove that there are matrices $A, B$ in $S$ such that
$AB$ is not a matrix in $S$. (Note: $A = B$ is allowed).
